author: jtraglia
content: Client devs, if you have time, I would appreciate more eyes on [this PR](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5294) which converts the lists of natural langage gossip validations to functions in Gloas. For the most part you can ignore the tests, we will review those. Please ensure (1) we're not forgetting any checks, (2) the order of the checks make sense, and (3) the executable check is actually doing what it says. Here's the important file: https://github.com/jtraglia/consensus-specs/blob/53e1de46d27c1b00e14e58a319038f9d3bd0e3a9/specs/gloas/p2p-interface.md#the-gossip-domain-gossipsub

author: luca_zanolini
content:
Hey all! We've been working on a security analysis of ePBS:

https://github.com/ethereum/epbs-security-analysis

The goal is to provide a self-contained reference for what ePBS does, the guarantees it provides, and the assumptions behind them. The analysis is intended to be faithful to the Gloas specs.

One of our objectives is also to make ePBS auditable by external parties who aren't necessarily fluent in the Python specs — the document presents the protocol and its properties at a level that a security reviewer can follow without first having to internalise the spec codebase.

We thought this would be valuable given that the specs are still WIP and ePBS has not shipped yet.

At this stage, feedback on Sections 1–8 would be the most useful: the protocol description, the six properties P1–P6 (Section 4), the adversarial analysis, and the different phases of ePBS. Section 9 contains proof sketches, but it is still WIP; we would rather get the earlier sections right before tightening the proofs around them.

If you spot anything off — a missed attack, an incorrect claim, an unclear definition, or anything else — please open an issue, or ping me or Roberto here.

There is also a much more technical companion document in the formal-treatment branch, but that is still heavily WIP and not ready for outside review yet.

Thanks!

author: potuz
content:
This is very cool that you guys are doing this. 

Just started reading. The 
"Each beacon block under ePBS carries two parent pointers, not one."  Concept I think it's erroneous or at least misleading. It gives the idea that two different chains have to be tracked independently or that these two pointers can kind of widely vary. 

I at least picture the forkchoice tree as if if node has a **single** parent, that is, the FC tree is indeed a tree. That single parent can be either full or empty, and that's about it. It's easier to reason about it this way instead of talking about parent root and parent hash

author: potuz
content:
In particular, this concept creeps into future sections like in 

"FULL/EMPTY is retrospective. $B$'s status is decided at slot N+1"

This is also not the right way of thinking about nodes IMO. I think as soon as the CL block B appears the node that is created is (B, empty). If a Payload is revealed a contending node is created which is (B, Full). These two nodes exist regardless of the chain or of any child

author: potuz
content: A child just chooses between them, just as we do today

author: luca_zanolini
content: This is about the data layout of a beacon block, not the fork-choice tree. The fork-choice tree (§6) is still a tree where each node has one parent; the two pointers below are two hash fields inside one block: the hash of the parent beacon block and the hash of the parent execution payload. The point of the two-pointer framing is that ePBS exposes two chains (the beacon chain and the execution chain) which can drift apart when a builder withholds.

author: luca_zanolini
content: We wanted to distinguish the data layout from the logical structure used by the FC

author: luca_zanolini
content: The mental model you are describing is exactly what Section 6 contains I think. The retrospective nature we are describing there is about a different object (on chain status) that can be evaluated by reading thr child block’s bid.parent_block_hash . FC tree != on chain status

author: potuz
content: Right but drifting apart is heavily constrained, it's either full or empty and not anything else. That's why the *two pointers* approach I believe is misleading as it hides this binary options
